title: Vitamin C brightening face serum
subtitle: A 10% L-ascorbic acid serum with niacinamide and hyaluronic acid at pH 3 to 3.5
excerpt: L-ascorbic acid is the most studied form of vitamin C in skincare but also the most demanding to formulate. It degrades rapidly in the presence of light, heat, and an incorrect pH. This serum is effective only if the pH is correct and the product is stored in an amber bottle away from light.
sourceNotes: L-ascorbic acid stability and pH requirements from Journal of Cosmetic Dermatology and standard cosmetic chemistry references. Niacinamide and L-ascorbic acid interaction data from peer-reviewed literature.

recipe.batchNotes: Make in small batches only. L-ascorbic acid degrades quickly; a 30 ml batch is the maximum sensible quantity.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Use within 3 months. Discard at first sign of yellowing or oranging of the serum.
recipe.temperatureNote: All ingredients must be at room temperature. Add L-ascorbic acid last, below 40 degrees Celsius, to prevent degradation.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 months from date of making, stored in an amber or opaque bottle. Discard immediately if the serum turns yellow, orange, or brown; oxidised vitamin C is no longer effective and may irritate skin.

recipeTools.notes: Accurate to 0.01 g for small batches
Essential; light degrades L-ascorbic acid rapidly
Essential; the serum must be pH 3 to 3.5

glossaryTerms.definition: The active, water-soluble form of vitamin C used in cosmetic serums. It is highly effective at the 10 to 20% concentration range but requires a pH of 3 to 3.5 to remain stable and penetrate the skin. Above pH 3.5 it converts to a less active form.
A humectant polysaccharide that attracts and holds water molecules at the skin surface. Used at 0.1 to 1 g per 100 ml of serum for a noticeable plumping effect.
Vitamin B3. An evidence-backed active used in serums for its effects on skin tone, pore appearance, and barrier function. Used at 2 to 5% in this serum.
The process of raising or lowering the pH of a cosmetic formula using acids (citric acid) or bases (sodium hydroxide solution). A vitamin C serum must be adjusted to pH 3 to 3.5 for efficacy.

The chemical degradation of L-ascorbic acid on exposure to air, light, and heat. Oxidised vitamin C turns yellow then orange then brown and is no longer active. An amber bottle and cool, dark storage slow but do not prevent oxidation.

items.prepNote: Dissolve in the distilled water first; 0.1 g per 100 ml target
10% of formula; add last, below 40 degrees Celsius
Added in small increments to achieve pH 3 to 3.5; typically 0.2 to 0.5 g

content.text: Dissolve the hyaluronic acid powder in the distilled water first, stirring well. It takes a few minutes to fully hydrate. Add the niacinamide powder and stir until dissolved. Add the preservative and stir.

powder and stir until fully dissolved. The solution will become slightly acidic on its own. Check the pH with a pH meter or strips. Use citric acid in tiny increments to bring the pH to the target range of 3 to 3.5 if needed. Stir thoroughly after each addition.
Funnel into the amber dropper bottle immediately. Label with the date made.

Store in the amber bottle in a cool, dark place, ideally a bathroom cabinet rather than a windowsill. Shelf life is 3 months. Discard at the first sign of yellowing or colour change; oxidised vitamin C is inactive. A fresh serum should be clear to very pale straw-coloured.
